segments.text: Manchester, in the heart of the north-west of England, is tonight host to a British political first.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: I'm Alasdair Stewart, and for the first time on British television, live in front of a representative studio audience, we'll be hearing from three men, each hoping to be the leader of the next UK government, in the first election debate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Good evening and welcome to the first election debate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Over the past 50 years, there have been numerous attempts to get the leaders of the three biggest political parties to debate with each other during an election.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Tonight, history in the making, as we are joined by the leader of the Liberal Democrats, Nick Clegg, the Conservative Party leader, David Cameron, and the leader of the Labour Party, Gordon Brown.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: This debate will mainly focus on domestic affairs, important issues that affect our everyday lives.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: If you want to make your own comments and follow the debate online, you can do so by going to ITV.com.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Now, in a moment, we'll be taking questions from our studio audience, but first, let's hear a brief opening statement from each of the leaders, and we're going to start with Nick Clegg.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: I believe the way things are is not the way things have to be.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Now, you're going to be told tonight by these two that the only choice you can make is between two old parties who've been running things for years.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I'm here to persuade you that there is an alternative.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I think we have a fantastic opportunity to do things differently for once.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And if we do things differently, we can create the fair society, the fair country we all want.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: A fair tax system.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Better schools.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: An economy no longer held hostage by greedy bankers.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Decent, open politics.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Those are the changes I believe in.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I really wouldn't be standing here tonight if I didn't think they were all possible.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So don't let anyone tell you that the only choice is old politics.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We can do something new.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We can do something different this time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That's what I'm about.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That's what the Liberal Democrats offer.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: These are no ordinary times.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And this is no ordinary election.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: We've just been going through the biggest global financial crisis in our lives.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And we're moving from recession to recovery.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And I believe we're moving on a road to prosperity for all.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: Now, every promise you hear from each of us this evening depends on one thing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: A strong economy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: and this is the defining year.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: Get the decisions right now and we can have secure jobs, we can have standards of living rising and we can have everybody better off.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: Get the decisions wrong now and we could have a double-dip recession.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And because we believe in fairness, as we cut the deficit, over these next few years, we will protect your police, your National Health Service, and we will protect your schools.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I know what this job involves.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I look forward to putting my plan to you this evening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I think it's great we're having these debates and I hope they go some way to restore some of the faith and some of the trust into our politics because we badly need that once again in this country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: The expenses saga brought great shame on Parliament and I'm extremely sorry for everything that happened.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Your politicians, frankly all of us, let you down.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: There is a big choice at this election.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: We can go on as we are, or we can say no, Britain can do much better.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: We can deal with our debts.

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: And we can build a bigger society.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: They've done some good things, and I would keep those.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: But we need change, and it's that change that I want to help to lead.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Gentlemen, thank you very much indeed.

segments.text: There are about a million carers in this country who care, I think now, for 50 hours or more for their loved ones, for members of their family who need care.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: They are the unsung army of heroines and heroes that keep our society together.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: They desperately need some time for themselves, time to go on holiday.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Under our plans, what we could do immediately is give those million carers who care for the greatest length of time at least a week off.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: at least a week off, just to have a breather, spend some time on their own, go and visit friends, go on holiday, just have some time to themselves again.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But as I say, I think we've all got some ideas, but I don't think any of us, and you don't hear this from politicians very much, I don't think any of us, actually, if we're really honest with you, have got the perfect solution.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That's why I think this is so important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Let's, for once, put people before politics and come up with a solution that works for you and your family in the long run.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Well, we have tried to do something about respite care because there are six million carers in this country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I've met many of them and talked to them about their needs.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: One need is respite so that they can have a break, as Nick said, and we're introducing and have actually made provision for more measures for respite care.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: But the question I was asking about also
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: how he could be sure that he could be less worried about having to go into an old people's home or what would happen if he got ill later on in his life.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: But that's why the urgent needs measure that we're introducing from April next year is so important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: It means that you can stay in your own home, have the help that's available, the equipment but also the home helps and the health visitors and at the moment it has been means tested, now in future it will be free of charge.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I think one of the biggest things we must do as we try, and I think it's right to try and forge a consensus because this is a long-term issue we must deal with, is to try and give the carer and those they care for more power and control and influence over their lives.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Form an individual budget for each one, make sure that if they want to they can take that as a direct payment, that they can make decisions about the sort of respite they need.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: We tried to do this with my son and
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: when you try and get a direct payment system, so you're in charge of the money and you can try and get some help, it's unbelievably complicated.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: You've got to set up a separate bank account, you've got to read about four lever arch files and, you know, I found it testing enough and what someone who's recently had to start caring, who's under huge pressure, who may be getting ill because of what they're doing, to try and get direct payments, let's make it easier because we ought to be trusting people to do this.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I think the key is of course urgent needs payments for people so that they are sure they can stay in their own home.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I agree that we need to do more for carers.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I want carers to be able to manage their own budgets as well and that's something that we're really working on for the future as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: but we've got to find a solution to this big problem and the big problem is people don't want to have to make the choice between owning their home and getting the care they need and that's why in the next Parliament I think it's important to try with the Commission to reach a consensus on what the funding will be for the future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: Well, I think everybody would be very surprised that the last question of the evening should actually have flowed into so much consensus.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I just think it is one of those issues, a bit like public sector pensions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I also think the scale of the public sector deficit, it's one of those issues where I think if we could go introduce a new kind of politics into this country, not the old style of politics, we could actually come up with a solution that everybody could agree to, because I think you and your family would benefit from it so much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Gentlemen, it'll disappoint you and it'll disappoint many people, but we have come to the end of our debating time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: If I can just explain to you, while you mull over what you've heard already, what happens now is that each of them will have one and a half minutes to make their closing statement.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: The evening began with an opener.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: You've heard lots of cut and thrust about a number of issues, but now each of them have one and a half minutes to attempt to persuade you of their overall position.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Mr Clegg, you first.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Well, thanks for starters for sticking with us for a full 90 minutes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: What I've tried to show you this evening is that there is an alternative to the two old parties.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I know many of you think that all politicians are just the same.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I hope I've tried to show you that that just isn't true.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Whether it's on the questions from Alan on care, Jacqueline on crime, Helen on politics, Joel on schooling, Robert on the deficit, I believe we can answer all of those questions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I believe we can rise to all of those challenges if we say no to the old parties and yes to something new and something different.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And that's what I offer, that's what the Liberal Democrats offer.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So don't let them tell you that the only choice is between two old parties who've been playing pass the parcel with your government for 65 years now, making the same promises, breaking the same promises, making the same old mistakes over and over again.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Because I think, despite all the challenges, all the problems we have, I think we can be really hopeful about the future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I genuinely believe we can have a better, fairer country if we do things differently.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So give real change a chance.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Trust your instincts.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Support fairness.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Choose something different.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And that will give you and your family a better, fairer life.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You know, it's been a great opportunity to exchange ideas this evening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And I know we're not up against the X Factor or Britain's Got Talent, and I hope people have been able to stay with us in the exchange that we've had about the future policies of this country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I was really struck with a number of questions, but particularly the one from Robert about the future of his healthcare trust and about the jobs that may be at risk.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And I've got to come to this central problem that we've got at the moment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: We have got to make a decision now about how we secure the recovery this year.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: We've got to make a decision about whether we put funds into the economy or take funds out of the economy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: Now, I'm very clear, we mustn't make the mistakes of the 1930s or the 1980s when unemployment rose for five years after the official end of the recession.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: So we've got to make sure the money is in the economy this year so that the recovery is secure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And then we've got to make sure that as we cut the deficit, we are fair to our National Health Service, our policing, and fair to our schools.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And that's why the National Insurance Rise is necessary to protect our health service, our schools, and our police.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And I think it was very interesting, when David Cameron was asked, he couldn't give a guarantee that we are giving about the funding on schools.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: He couldn't give a guarantee about the funding on policing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: And when it came to the National Health Service, he couldn't give the same personal guarantees that we're giving about cancer specialist care, about seeing a GP at the evenings and weekends.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: Now these are problems he's got to address in the future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I look forward to the next debate so we can get all the issues raised, aired about the future of our country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: David Cameron.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_10

segments.text: I think it has been a great opportunity to have this debate and I think one of the things I've heard during this debate is just repeated attempts to try and frighten you about a Conservative government and I would say choose hope over fear.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: because we have incredibly exciting and optimistic plans for the future of our country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: A great vision where we build a bigger society, where we get our economy moving, where we stop Labour's jobs tax which could destroy that economy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I think it's been shown tonight, the idea you have to go on wasting money to secure the recovery is simply wrong.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: You've heard a lot about policy tonight, but I think as important as policy is your values.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Let me tell you mine.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: If you work hard, I'll be behind you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: If you want to raise a family, I will support you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: If you're old and you become ill, we will always be there for you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: This is an amazing country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: We've done incredible things.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I think we can go on and do even more incredible things.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: But we need two things, a government with the right values and also an understanding that we're all in this together and real change comes when we come together and work together.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: That's the sort of change and that's the sort of leadership that I would bring to our great country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Gentlemen, thank you very much indeed for your contributions to this, our first debate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: And can I turn to you as well, all of you here with us this evening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Thank you particularly to those who asked the questions and also a heartfelt thanks to those who submitted brilliant questions but for which we did not have time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Thank you so much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Our thanks, then, to Nick Clegg, to Gordon Brown and to David Cameron.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Stay with us now on ITV1 for instant reaction and analysis of the debate on News At Ten, including the results of the first opinion poll on who came out on top tonight.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: At the end of an historic moment in television and political history, a very good night to you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13

segments.text: Good night.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_13
